repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
I feel like the major development of `vim.Pos` and `vim.Range` is nearing completion. I have conducted a full review of the existing code and performed some refactoring. The following description is for the most significant one; other minor changes for different purposes are contained in a separate commit, with details provided in that commit message. 

## Problem
- To share logic, creating a `vim.Range` currently creates two `vim.Pos` values as intermediates, which causes unnecessary table allocations.
- `pos.lua` and `range.lua` contain some overlapping logic.

## Solution
Add `vim.pos._util`, a module for handling positions represented directly by `row` and `col`.

comment_data.body: Could you avoid having tiny changes like these one that are different from the main PR goals? It makes reviewing harder and makes it harder to track down when a change was introduced.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -348,7 +348,7 @@ function Completor:accept(item)
       api.nvim_buf_set_text(self.bufnr, start_row, start_col, end_row, end_col, lines)
       local win = api.nvim_get_current_win()
       win = api.nvim_win_get_buf(win) == self.bufnr and win or vim.fn.bufwinid(self.bufnr)
-      local row, col = item.range:to_cursor()
+      local row, col = item.range:to_mark()

comment_data.body: Instead of adding (auto-assigned) TODO comments like this one I would suggest creating an issue. This feels a bit like "licking the cookie" and preventing someone else from making the change.

---- 1: a > b
---- 0: a == b
---- -1: a < b

-    return cmp_pos(...) ~= 0
-  end,
-}
-
-setmetatable(M.cmp_pos, { __call = cmp_pos })
+-- TODO(ofseed): directly use `cmp_pos` from `util` and replace all exported usages.

comment_data.body:
The reason for placing it in this section is that `vim.Range`/`vim.Pos` are described as experimental in 0.12, and this section is intended for experimental features currently under development, which will be removed after the release of 0.13. 

I feel it would be confusing for users to see a feature that was already experimental being removed once 0.13 is released.

+• range.cursor() and range.to_cursor() are removed.
+  Use range.mark() and range.to_mark() instead.

comment_data.body:
The title of the PR is `refactor(pos,range): extract vim.pos._util`, and from reading the description I don't immediately see how this PR also includes the removal of `to_cursor`.

> It can be traced back to the removal of `range:to_cursor()` via git blame.

Commit history is our changelog. I don't want to trace back endless discussions via `git blame` to understand why code changed. This repo has a _wonderful_ Git history, let's try to preserve that :)

-      local row, col = item.range:to_cursor()
+      local row, col = item.range:to_mark()

comment_data.body:
I am actually keeping the history clean; this PR consists of three commits. Through `git blame`, this can trace back to a commit titled "feat(pos,range)!: handle cursor position/range more consistently," which includes a specific Problem/Solution section describing why it was removed. I recommend reviewing this PR commit by commit.

The reason I didn't split these three commits into three separate PRs is that they have a chronological dependency. The first commit removes `range.cursor()`/`range:to_cursor()`, but the second commit moves the implementation of all `range.xxx()`/`range:to_xxx()` functions to `pos/util.lua`. Splitting them into two PRs would mean I'd have to perform the same refactoring for `range.cursor()`/`range:to_cursor()` as well. The same applies to the third commit; splitting it off separately would mean I'd have to improve the documentation for functions I already intended to remove.

[stacked PRs](https://github.github.com/gh-stack/) could solve this problem, but until then, I can only include them in a single PR in sequential order, each with its own clean commit message. If I were to split the PR, in addition to the redundant implementation mentioned above, I would also have to resolve conflicts arising from whichever commit gets merged first.

comment_data.body: could use a brief docstring, else this is a bit confusing. I think I understand that `row` is different than `lnum`, but that's subtle.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,198 @@
+---@brief
+--- Unlike `vim.pos`, this module is used to provide utility functions
+--- for unpacked `row`, `col`.
+
+local api = vim.api
+local uv = vim.uv
+
+local M = {}
+
+---@param a_row integer
+---@param a_col integer
+---@param b_row integer
+---@param b_col integer
+---@return integer
+--- 1: a > b
+--- 0: a == b
+--- -1: a < b
+local function cmp_pos(a_row, a_col, b_row, b_col)
+  if a_row == b_row then
+    if a_col > b_col then
+      return 1
+    elseif a_col < b_col then
+      return -1
+    else
+      return 0
+    end
+  elseif a_row > b_row then
+    return 1
+  end
+
+  return -1
+end
+
+---@type table<'lt'|'le'|'gt'|'ge'|'eq'|'ne', fun(a_row: integer, a_col: integer, b_row: integer, b_col: integer): boolean>
+M.cmp_pos = {
+  lt = function(...)
+    return cmp_pos(...) == -1
+  end,
+  le = function(...)
+    return cmp_pos(...) ~= 1
+  end,
+  gt = function(...)
+    return cmp_pos(...) == 1
+  end,
+  ge = function(...)
+    return cmp_pos(...) ~= -1
+  end,
+  eq = function(...)
+    return cmp_pos(...) == 0
+  end,
+  ne = function(...)
+    return cmp_pos(...) ~= 0
+  end,
+}
+
+setmetatable(M.cmp_pos, { __call = cmp_pos })
+
+--- Gets the zero-indexed lines from the given buffer.
+--- Works on unloaded buffers by reading the file using libuv to bypass buf reading events.
+--- Falls back to loading the buffer and nvim_buf_get_lines for buffers with non-file URI.
+---
+---@param bufnr integer bufnr to get the lines from
+---@param rows integer[] zero-indexed line numbers
+---@return table<integer, string> # a table mapping rows to lines
+function M.get_lines(bufnr, rows)
+  --- @type integer[]
+  rows = type(rows) == 'table' and rows or { rows }
+
+  -- This is needed for bufload and bufloaded
+  bufnr = vim._resolve_bufnr(bufnr)
+
+  local function buf_lines()
+    local lines = {} --- @type table<integer,string>
+    for _, row in ipairs(rows) do
+      lines[row] = (api.nvim_buf_get_lines(bufnr, row, row + 1, false) or { '' })[1]
+    end
+    return lines
+  end
+
+  -- use loaded buffers if available
+  if vim.fn.bufloaded(bufnr) == 1 then
+    return buf_lines()
+  end
+
+  local uri = vim.uri_from_bufnr(bufnr)
+
+  -- load the buffer if this is not a file uri
+  -- Custom language server protocol extensions can result in servers sending URIs with custom schemes. Plugins are able to load these via `BufReadCmd` autocmds.
+  if uri:sub(1, 4) ~= 'file' then
+    vim.fn.bufload(bufnr)
+    return buf_lines()
+  end
+
+  local filename = api.nvim_buf_get_name(bufnr)
+  if vim.fn.isdirectory(filename) ~= 0 then
+    return {}
+  end
+
+  -- get the data from the file
+  local fd = uv.fs_open(filename, 'r', 438)
+  if not fd then
+    return {}
+  end
+  local stat = assert(uv.fs_fstat(fd))
+  local data = assert(uv.fs_read(fd, stat.size, 0))
+  uv.fs_close(fd)
+
+  local lines = {} --- @type table<integer,true|string> rows we need to retrieve
+  local need = 0 -- keep track of how many unique rows we need
+  for _, row in pairs(rows) do
+    if not lines[row] then
+      need = need + 1
+    end
+    lines[row] = true
+  end
+
+  local found = 0
+  local lnum = 0
+
+  for line in string.gmatch(data, '([^\n]*)\n?') do
+    if lines[lnum] == true then
+      lines[lnum] = line
+      found = found + 1
+      if found == need then
+        break
+      end
+    end
+    lnum = lnum + 1
+  end
+
+  -- change any lines we didn't find to the empty string
+  for i, line in pairs(lines) do
+    if line == true then
+      lines[i] = ''
+    end
+  end
+  return lines --[[@as table<integer,string>]]
+end
+
+--- Gets the zero-indexed line from the given buffer.
+--- Works on unloaded buffers by reading the file using libuv to bypass buf reading events.
+--- Falls back to loading the buffer and nvim_buf_get_lines for buffers with non-file URI.
+---
+---@param bufnr integer
+---@param row integer zero-indexed line number
+---@return string the line at row in filename
+function M.get_line(bufnr, row)
+  return M.get_lines(bufnr, { row })[row]
+end
+
+---@param buf integer
+---@param row integer
+---@param col integer
+---@param position_encoding lsp.PositionEncodingKind
+function M.to_lsp(buf, row, col, position_encoding)
+  -- When on the first character,
+  -- we can ignore the difference between byte and character.
+  if col > 0 then
+    col = vim.str_utfindex(M.get_line(buf, row), position_encoding, col, false)
+  elseif col == 0 and row == api.nvim_buf_line_count(buf) and not vim.bo[buf].endofline then
+    -- Some LSP servers reject ranges that end at the virtual EOF position
+    -- (i.e., `[line_count, 0]`) when the buffer has no trailing newline.
+    -- Normalize such positions to the end of the last real line instead.
+    row = row - 1
+    col = vim.str_utfindex(M.get_line(buf, row), position_encoding)
+  end
+  ---@type lsp.Position
+  return { line = row, character = col }
+end
+
+---@param buf integer
+---@param position lsp.Position
+---@param position_encoding lsp.PositionEncodingKind
+function M.from_lsp(buf, position, position_encoding)
+  local row, col = position.line, position.character
+  -- When on the first character,
+  -- we can ignore the difference between byte and character.
+  if col > 0 then
+    -- `strict_indexing` is disabled, because LSP responses are asynchronous,
+    -- and the buffer content may have changed, causing out-of-bounds errors.
+    col = vim.str_byteindex(M.get_line(buf, row) or '', position_encoding, col, false)
+  end
+  return row, col
+end
+
+---@param row integer
+---@param col integer
+function M.to_mark(row, col)

comment_data.body: I updated the PR title. The changes make sense, reading the commit messages also helps
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -348,7 +348,7 @@ function Completor:accept(item)
       api.nvim_buf_set_text(self.bufnr, start_row, start_col, end_row, end_col, lines)
       local win = api.nvim_get_current_win()
       win = api.nvim_win_get_buf(win) == self.bufnr and win or vim.fn.bufwinid(self.bufnr)
-      local row, col = item.range:to_cursor()
+      local row, col = item.range:to_mark()

comment_data.body:
This is consistent with `pos.cursor()`, which accepts a table containing `row` and `col` rather than two separate arguments, and is inconsistent with other `pos.xxx` too. I prefer consistency with `pos.cursor()` over `pos.to_xxx()`; instead, a pos created by `pos.cursor()` cannot be converted back to its original type via `pos.to_cursor()` without manual wrapping, which could be a pitfall.

If you downvote this comment, I will revert this change.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -4560,22 +4597,36 @@ offset({buf}, {offset})                                     *vim.pos.offset()*
 to_cursor({pos})                                         *vim.pos.to_cursor()*
     Converts |vim.Pos| to cursor position (see |api-indexing|).
 
+    Example: >lua
+        local pos = vim.pos(0, 3, 5)
+
+        -- Convert to cursor position, you can call it in a method style.
+        local cursor_pos = pos:to_cursor()
+        vim.api.nvim_win_set_cursor(0, cursor_pos)
+<
+
     Parameters: ~
       • {pos}  (`vim.Pos`) See |vim.Pos|.
 
-    Return (multiple): ~
-        (`integer`)
-        (`integer`)
+    Return: ~
+        (`[integer, integer]`) (row, col) tuple

comment_data.body:
I will add the docstrings.

> I think I understand that `row` is different than `lnum`, but that's subtle.

This might be a bit off-topic, but there are some inconsistencies in the current code regarding variable names for line numbers; they are variously called `row`, `lnum`, or `line`. Without introducing breaking changes, I would prefer to represent them as follows:

- `row` is used to represent a 0-based index.
- `lnum` is short for "line number," used to represent a 1-based index.
- `line` is used to represent the text of a specific line; that is, `line` is always a string.

What do you think about the implications of this naming?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,198 @@
+---@brief
+--- Unlike `vim.pos`, this module is used to provide utility functions
+--- for unpacked `row`, `col`.
+
+local api = vim.api
+local uv = vim.uv
+
+local M = {}
+
+---@param a_row integer
+---@param a_col integer
+---@param b_row integer
+---@param b_col integer
+---@return integer
+--- 1: a > b
+--- 0: a == b
+--- -1: a < b
+local function cmp_pos(a_row, a_col, b_row, b_col)
+  if a_row == b_row then
+    if a_col > b_col then
+      return 1
+    elseif a_col < b_col then
+      return -1
+    else
+      return 0
+    end
+  elseif a_row > b_row then
+    return 1
+  end
+
+  return -1
+end
+
+---@type table<'lt'|'le'|'gt'|'ge'|'eq'|'ne', fun(a_row: integer, a_col: integer, b_row: integer, b_col: integer): boolean>
+M.cmp_pos = {
+  lt = function(...)
+    return cmp_pos(...) == -1
+  end,
+  le = function(...)
+    return cmp_pos(...) ~= 1
+  end,
+  gt = function(...)
+    return cmp_pos(...) == 1
+  end,
+  ge = function(...)
+    return cmp_pos(...) ~= -1
+  end,
+  eq = function(...)
+    return cmp_pos(...) == 0
+  end,
+  ne = function(...)
+    return cmp_pos(...) ~= 0
+  end,
+}
+
+setmetatable(M.cmp_pos, { __call = cmp_pos })
+
+--- Gets the zero-indexed lines from the given buffer.
+--- Works on unloaded buffers by reading the file using libuv to bypass buf reading events.
+--- Falls back to loading the buffer and nvim_buf_get_lines for buffers with non-file URI.
+---
+---@param bufnr integer bufnr to get the lines from
+---@param rows integer[] zero-indexed line numbers
+---@return table<integer, string> # a table mapping rows to lines
+function M.get_lines(bufnr, rows)
+  --- @type integer[]
+  rows = type(rows) == 'table' and rows or { rows }
+
+  -- This is needed for bufload and bufloaded
+  bufnr = vim._resolve_bufnr(bufnr)
+
+  local function buf_lines()
+    local lines = {} --- @type table<integer,string>
+    for _, row in ipairs(rows) do
+      lines[row] = (api.nvim_buf_get_lines(bufnr, row, row + 1, false) or { '' })[1]
+    end
+    return lines
+  end
+
+  -- use loaded buffers if available
+  if vim.fn.bufloaded(bufnr) == 1 then
+    return buf_lines()
+  end
+
+  local uri = vim.uri_from_bufnr(bufnr)
+
+  -- load the buffer if this is not a file uri
+  -- Custom language server protocol extensions can result in servers sending URIs with custom schemes. Plugins are able to load these via `BufReadCmd` autocmds.
+  if uri:sub(1, 4) ~= 'file' then
+    vim.fn.bufload(bufnr)
+    return buf_lines()
+  end
+
+  local filename = api.nvim_buf_get_name(bufnr)
+  if vim.fn.isdirectory(filename) ~= 0 then
+    return {}
+  end
+
+  -- get the data from the file
+  local fd = uv.fs_open(filename, 'r', 438)
+  if not fd then
+    return {}
+  end
+  local stat = assert(uv.fs_fstat(fd))
+  local data = assert(uv.fs_read(fd, stat.size, 0))
+  uv.fs_close(fd)
+
+  local lines = {} --- @type table<integer,true|string> rows we need to retrieve
+  local need = 0 -- keep track of how many unique rows we need
+  for _, row in pairs(rows) do
+    if not lines[row] then
+      need = need + 1
+    end
+    lines[row] = true
+  end
+
+  local found = 0
+  local lnum = 0
+
+  for line in string.gmatch(data, '([^\n]*)\n?') do
+    if lines[lnum] == true then
+      lines[lnum] = line
+      found = found + 1
+      if found == need then
+        break
+      end
+    end
+    lnum = lnum + 1
+  end
+
+  -- change any lines we didn't find to the empty string
+  for i, line in pairs(lines) do
+    if line == true then
+      lines[i] = ''
+    end
+  end
+  return lines --[[@as table<integer,string>]]
+end
+
+--- Gets the zero-indexed line from the given buffer.
+--- Works on unloaded buffers by reading the file using libuv to bypass buf reading events.
+--- Falls back to loading the buffer and nvim_buf_get_lines for buffers with non-file URI.
+---
+---@param bufnr integer
+---@param row integer zero-indexed line number
+---@return string the line at row in filename
+function M.get_line(bufnr, row)
+  return M.get_lines(bufnr, { row })[row]
+end
+
+---@param buf integer
+---@param row integer
+---@param col integer
+---@param position_encoding lsp.PositionEncodingKind
+function M.to_lsp(buf, row, col, position_encoding)
+  -- When on the first character,
+  -- we can ignore the difference between byte and character.
+  if col > 0 then
+    col = vim.str_utfindex(M.get_line(buf, row), position_encoding, col, false)
+  elseif col == 0 and row == api.nvim_buf_line_count(buf) and not vim.bo[buf].endofline then
+    -- Some LSP servers reject ranges that end at the virtual EOF position
+    -- (i.e., `[line_count, 0]`) when the buffer has no trailing newline.
+    -- Normalize such positions to the end of the last real line instead.
+    row = row - 1
+    col = vim.str_utfindex(M.get_line(buf, row), position_encoding)
+  end
+  ---@type lsp.Position
+  return { line = row, character = col }
+end
+
+---@param buf integer
+---@param position lsp.Position
+---@param position_encoding lsp.PositionEncodingKind
+function M.from_lsp(buf, position, position_encoding)
+  local row, col = position.line, position.character
+  -- When on the first character,
+  -- we can ignore the difference between byte and character.
+  if col > 0 then
+    -- `strict_indexing` is disabled, because LSP responses are asynchronous,
+    -- and the buffer content may have changed, causing out-of-bounds errors.
+    col = vim.str_byteindex(M.get_line(buf, row) or '', position_encoding, col, false)
+  end
+  return row, col
+end
+
+---@param row integer
+---@param col integer
+function M.to_mark(row, col)

comment_data.body: Or `vim.pos.is_valid`, may have a better implication and be more versatile.

@@ -4539,17 +4554,39 @@ lsp({buf}, {pos}, {position_encoding})                         *vim.pos.lsp()*
       • {pos}                (`lsp.Position`)
       • {position_encoding}  (`lsp.PositionEncodingKind`)
 
-mark({buf}, {row}, {col})                                     *vim.pos.mark()*
+    Return: ~
+        (`vim.Pos`) See |vim.Pos|.
+
+mark({buf}, {lnum}, {col})                                    *vim.pos.mark()*
     Creates a new |vim.Pos| from mark position (see |api-indexing|).
 
+    Example: >lua
+        local mark_info = vim.api.nvim_get_mark('M', {})
+        local lnum, col, buf, name = unpack(mark_info)
+
+        if lnum == 0 and col == 0 and buf == 0 then
+          return -- mark 'M' is not set.

comment_data.body:
> consistent with `pos.cursor()`, which accepts a table containing `row` and `col` rather than two separate arguments

Maybe we should change it to accept separate args? I know that was intentionally decided already, essentially to support `pos.cursor(0, nvim_win_get_cursor())`, but isn't it likely that we will add ergonomics such as `pos.cursor(0)` which get "current cursor" automatically?

We can consider this as a followup, though, doesn't need to blocker this PR.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -4560,22 +4597,36 @@ offset({buf}, {offset})                                     *vim.pos.offset()*
 to_cursor({pos})                                         *vim.pos.to_cursor()*
     Converts |vim.Pos| to cursor position (see |api-indexing|).
 
+    Example: >lua
+        local pos = vim.pos(0, 3, 5)
+
+        -- Convert to cursor position, you can call it in a method style.
+        local cursor_pos = pos:to_cursor()
+        vim.api.nvim_win_set_cursor(0, cursor_pos)
+<
+
     Parameters: ~
       • {pos}  (`vim.Pos`) See |vim.Pos|.
 
-    Return (multiple): ~
-        (`integer`)
-        (`integer`)
+    Return: ~
+        (`[integer, integer]`) (row, col) tuple

comment_data.body: SGTM, let's be sure to document that in the `@brief` too.

comment_regular_data.body:
Orthogonal, but since you're adding user-facing documentation: Can you add an overview section to `vim.pos` (with its own tag) that compares all(!) the different indexing conventions (0 v. 1-based, byte- v. char-based, end-inclusive v. exclusive, EOL inclusive v. exclusive) in a handy table? This should include the "native `vim.Pos`, which right now is only documented as being "well-defined".

Same for `vim.range`, I guess.

> Can you add an overview section to `vim.pos` (with its own tag) that compares all(!) the different indexing conventions (0 v. 1-based, byte- v. char-based, end-inclusive v. exclusive, EOL inclusive v. exclusive) in a handy table?

This is partially described in `:h api-indexing`; perhaps it would be better to update the documentation there? I tried adding a table similar to the one under `:h setg` or an ASCII table at the beginning of `:h vim.pos`, but `gen_vimdoc.lua` throws an error. It seems better to do this in a separate PR(becasue need to updating `:h api-indexing` in `api.txt` or modify `gen_vimdoc.lua`)

No, I meant what I wrote. `:h api-indexing` is not specific enough, and the documentation should be where it matters (namely, where people see all the different conversion functions and wonder which one they need) and in one place (including for LSP and treesitter!)
the docs update can be a followup?

> the docs update can be a followup?

Are you referring to the supplementary mentioned by clason rather than the third commit in this PR? If so, I will supplement the documentation at the beginning of `vim.pos` and `vim.range` in a follow-up, including:
- Listing all position types and comparing their indexing methods in a table.
- Describing the implications of `lnum` and `row` regarding the index base.
- (Perhaps) describing `vim.Pos` and `vim.Range` must be associated with a buffer, as I mentioned in https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39867#issuecomment-4485570263.
- (Perhaps) updating `gen_vimdoc.lua` so that it can correctly generate tables from Lua comments into vimdoc; otherwise, we would have to write the introductory sections of `vim.pos`/`vim.range` directly in `lua.txt`.

Then, treat the third commit here as a supplement to each function within the module, excluding changes to the overview information.
Table format is not crucial, only the information matters. Bullet point list is fine, too.

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

labels.description: changes that are not features or bugfixes
labels.name: refactor

title: refactor(pos,range): drop `to_cursor`, extract `vim.pos._util`
